title: Ephesians 3
subtitle: The Mystery Revealed and the Prayer for Power

heb.paragraph: In this passage mystērion receives its fullest definition: ‘the Gentiles are heirs together with Israel, members together of one body, and sharers together in the promise in Christ Jesus’ (v. 6). Three syn- compound words (synklēronoma, syssōma, symmetocha) hammer home the togetherness: co-heirs, co-bodied, co-sharers. The mystery is not that Gentiles would be saved (the OT anticipated this), but that they would be fully incorporated into the same body as Israel without becoming Jews.

The noun oikonomia (vv. 2, 9) denotes the administration or management of a household. Paul has received the stewardship of God’s grace to make the mystery known to the Gentiles. In v. 9, the cosmic scope emerges: God’s oikonomia is the administration of the mystery hidden for ages but now revealed through the church.

refs.note: Paul’s parallel description of the mystery in Colossians defines it as ‘Christ in you, the hope of glory.’ Ephesians emphasises the corporate dimension: Gentiles included in the same body.
Paul’s doxology at the end of Romans celebrates ‘the mystery hidden for long ages past, but now revealed,’ using the same revelatory schema as Eph 3:3–5, 9.

notes.note: Paul’s imprisonment is ‘for the sake of you Gentiles’—his arrest was triggered by his Gentile mission (Acts 21:28–29). The mystery was made known by revelation (v. 3), not by human investigation. The three syn- compounds of v. 6 are Paul’s most emphatic statement of Gentile equality in the body of Christ.

Paul describes himself as ‘less than the least of all the Lord’s people’ (v. 8)—a superlative of humility (elachistoteros, a comparative form of a superlative). The purpose of his ministry is that ‘through the church, the manifold wisdom of God should be made known to the rulers and authorities.’ MacArthur notes the staggering implication: the church is God’s visual aid to the cosmos, displaying his wisdom to angelic and demonic powers.

Calvin emphasises that the mystery was not entirely unknown in the OT (the prophets anticipated Gentile inclusion) but was not known ‘as it has now been revealed’—the full scope and manner of Gentile incorporation was a new revelation. The mystery is not mere inclusion but equal participation: co-heirs, co-members, co-sharers.

Calvin reads this verse as the ultimate purpose of the church’s existence: to display God’s manifold wisdom to the cosmic powers. The adjective polypoikilos (manifold, many-coloured, variegated) suggests that God’s wisdom is not monochrome but richly diverse, displayed in the multi-ethnic, multi-cultural unity of the church.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The three syn- compounds (synklēronoma, syssōma, symmetocha) are intensely emphatic. The NET translators note that syssōma (fellow members of the body) is found only here in Greek literature and may be a Pauline coinage.

The adjective polypoikilos (manifold, multifaceted) appears only here in the NT. In classical Greek it described elaborate embroidery or the varied colours of flowers and marble, suggesting rich diversity and intricate beauty.

lincoln.source: Andrew T. Lincoln, Ephesians, WBC (1990) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lincoln reads the digression as Paul’s self-understanding of his role in salvation history. He is not merely a preacher but a steward of the mystery, appointed to make known what was hidden for ages. Lincoln notes the cosmic ecclesiology of v. 10: the church’s unity is not merely a social achievement but a theological demonstration—the cosmic powers learn God’s wisdom by observing what God has done in the church.

obrien.source: Peter T. O'Brien, Ephesians, Pillar (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Brien identifies the passage’s structure as a revelation schema: formerly hidden (vv. 5, 9) / now revealed (vv. 3, 5, 10). The mystery’s content (Gentile co-incorporation) and its cosmic purpose (display of divine wisdom to heavenly powers) together define the church’s significance. O’Brien argues that the ‘rulers and authorities’ of v. 10 include both angelic and demonic powers, for whom the church’s existence is evidence of God’s sovereignty.

hist.context: Ephesians 3 is structured as an interrupted prayer. Paul begins ‘For this reason I, Paul, the prisoner of Christ Jesus for the sake of you Gentiles’ (v. 1), then digresses into a description of his ministry and the mystery revealed to him (vv. 2–13), before resuming the prayer in v. 14 (‘For this reason I kneel before the Father’). The digression is not parenthetical but essential: Paul explains why the mystery matters. The content of the mystery (v. 6) is the full incorporation of Gentiles into the people of God. The purpose of the mystery’s revelation through the church is cosmic: ‘through the church, the manifold wisdom of God should be made known to the rulers and authorities in the heavenly realms’ (v. 10). The church is not merely the beneficiary of God’s wisdom but its demonstration to cosmic powers.

heb.paragraph: The noun plērōma (v. 19) is the prayer’s climactic goal: that believers ‘may be filled to the measure of all the fullness of God.’ This is the most audacious petition in the Pauline corpus. The fullness of God is not something believers achieve but something God pours into them through the Spirit’s power and Christ’s indwelling.

refs.note: Paul’s declaration that ‘in Christ all the fullness of the Deity lives in bodily form, and in Christ you have been brought to fullness’ provides the christological basis for the petition in Eph 3:19.
Paul’s conviction that nothing can separate believers from God’s love parallels the prayer’s focus on comprehending the dimensions of Christ’s love (Eph 3:18–19).

notes.note: Paul kneels—the posture of supplication, suggesting urgent, intense prayer. The petition for inner strengthening through the Spirit acknowledges that the Christian life is not lived in human power but in the Spirit’s power operating in the ‘inner being.’

The four dimensions of Christ’s love (breadth, length, height, depth) are not precise measurements but a rhetorical way of saying that Christ’s love exceeds all measurement. Yet Paul prays that believers would know this love ‘that surpasses knowledge’—a deliberate paradox: experiential knowledge of a love too vast for intellectual comprehension.

The doxology is among the most exalted in Scripture. God’s power exceeds not only what we ask but what we imagine. MacArthur notes the escalation: more than we ask, more than we think, immeasurably more than both.

Calvin notes that each petition builds on the previous: the Spirit’s strengthening enables Christ’s indwelling, which enables love’s comprehension, which leads to fullness. The goal—being filled with God’s fullness—does not mean becoming God but being filled with all the graces and blessings God communicates through his Spirit.

Calvin reads the doxology as a summary of the letter’s first half: God’s power in salvation exceeds all human imagination. The phrase ‘in the church and in Christ Jesus’ yokes ecclesiology and Christology: God is glorified in the church precisely because the church is in Christ.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The four dimensions (platos, mēkos, hypsos, bathos) have no stated object: the love of Christ? The mystery? The purpose of God? The NET translators connect them to ‘the love of Christ’ (v. 19), though the cosmic scope may intentionally leave the referent open.
The compound adverb hyperekperissou (immeasurably more, far beyond) is one of Paul’s most emphatic superlatives, combining hyper (beyond), ek (out of), and perissou (abundantly).

lincoln.source: Andrew T. Lincoln, Ephesians, WBC (1990) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lincoln reads the prayer as the spiritual counterpart to the theological exposition of 1:3–3:13. Having laid out God’s cosmic purpose, Paul prays that the readers will experience it personally. The four-dimensional language (v. 18) likely draws on cosmological imagery: the love of Christ spans the entire created order. The doxology (vv. 20–21) serves as the hinge between the letter’s doctrinal (chs. 1–3) and ethical (chs. 4–6) halves.

obrien.source: Peter T. O'Brien, Ephesians, Pillar (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Brien identifies this as one of the greatest prayers in the NT. Its structure is concentric: the outer frame is the Father (v. 14) and the doxology (vv. 20–21), the inner core is the indwelling of Christ and the comprehension of his love (vv. 17–19). O’Brien notes that the prayer moves from empowerment (v. 16) through indwelling (v. 17) to comprehension (v. 18) to fullness (v. 19)—an ascending trajectory of spiritual experience culminating in the fullness of God.

hist.context: Paul resumes the prayer interrupted at v. 1. He kneels before the Father (v. 14)—an unusual posture (Jews typically stood to pray)—suggesting the intensity and reverence of the petition. The prayer has four requests: (1) that the Father would strengthen them with power through his Spirit in the inner being (v. 16); (2) that Christ may dwell in their hearts through faith (v. 17a); (3) that they, being rooted and established in love, may have power to grasp the dimensions of Christ’s love (vv. 17b–19a); (4) that they may be filled to the measure of all the fullness of God (v. 19b). The prayer concludes with a doxology (vv. 20–21) celebrating God’s power to do ‘immeasurably more than all we ask or imagine.’

rows.label: vv.2–6
rows.text: The mystery defined: Gentiles as co-heirs, co-body, co-sharers

rows.label: vv.7–9
rows.text: Paul’s commission: to preach unsearchable riches to Gentiles

rows.label: vv.10–13
rows.text: Cosmic purpose: the church displays God’s wisdom to heavenly powers

rows.label: vv.14–16
rows.text: Prayer resumes: strengthened by the Spirit in the inner being

rows.label: vv.17–19
rows.text: Christ’s indwelling; love’s four dimensions; filled with God’s fullness

rows.label: vv.20–21
rows.text: Doxology: immeasurably more than all we ask or imagine

lit.note: Ephesians 3 reveals the content of the mystery (Gentile co-incorporation) and its cosmic purpose (displaying God’s wisdom to heavenly powers), then concludes with a prayer for experiential apprehension of Christ’s love and a doxology celebrating God’s surpassing power.

themes.note: Sovereignty and Worship share the highest scores: the mystery reveals God’s sovereign cosmic plan, and the chapter climaxes with one of Scripture’s greatest doxologies. Mission is prominent through Paul’s commission to preach the unsearchable riches of Christ. Mercy underlies the mystery’s content: Gentile inclusion by grace.

hebtext.note: In this passage mystērion receives its fullest definition: ‘the Gentiles are heirs together with Israel, members together of one body, and sharers together in the promise in Christ Jesus’ (v. 6). Three syn- compound words (synklēronoma, syssōma, symmetocha) hammer home the togetherness: co-heirs, co-bodied, co-sharers. The mystery is not that Gentiles would be saved (the OT anticipated this), but that they would be fully incorporated into the same body as Israel without becoming Jews.

The noun oikonomia (vv. 2, 9) denotes the administration or management of a household. Paul has received the stewardship of God’s grace to make the mystery known to the Gentiles. In v. 9, the cosmic scope emerges: God’s oikonomia is the administration of the mystery hidden for ages but now revealed through the church.

The noun plērōma (v. 19) is the prayer’s climactic goal: that believers ‘may be filled to the measure of all the fullness of God.’ This is the most audacious petition in the Pauline corpus. The fullness of God is not something believers achieve but something God pours into them through the Spirit’s power and Christ’s indwelling.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Mystery Revealed and the Prayer for Power

translations.text: This mystery is that through the gospel the Gentiles are heirs together with Israel, members together of one body, and sharers together in the promise in Christ Jesus.
This mystery is that the Gentiles are fellow heirs, members of the same body, and partakers of the promise in Christ Jesus through the gospel.
and to make plain to everyone the administration of this mystery, which for ages past was kept hidden in God, who created all things.
and to bring to light for everyone what is the plan of the mystery hidden for ages in God, who created all things,

thread.anchor: The Mystery Revealed and the Prayer for Power
thread.text: Paul’s parallel description of the mystery in Colossians defines it as ‘Christ in you, the hope of glory.’ Ephesians emphasises the corporate dimension: Gentiles included in the same body.

thread.anchor: The Mystery Revealed and the Prayer for Power
thread.text: Paul’s doxology at the end of Romans celebrates ‘the mystery hidden for long ages past, but now revealed,’ using the same revelatory schema as Eph 3:3–5, 9.

thread.anchor: The Mystery Revealed and the Prayer for Power
thread.text: Paul’s declaration that ‘in Christ all the fullness of the Deity lives in bodily form, and in Christ you have been brought to fullness’ provides the christological basis for the petition in Eph 3:19.

thread.anchor: The Mystery Revealed and the Prayer for Power
thread.text: Paul’s conviction that nothing can separate believers from God’s love parallels the prayer’s focus on comprehending the dimensions of Christ’s love (Eph 3:18–19).

discourse.thesis: The mystery of Christ — that Gentiles are fellow heirs and members of the same body — was hidden for ages but is now revealed through Paul's ministry, to display the wisdom of God to the cosmic powers.

nodes.text: Paul begins an intercession (completed only in v. 14 — a long parenthetical), first explaining his role. He is a prisoner of Christ Jesus on behalf of the Gentiles — his imprisonment is not failure but the shape of apostolic vocation. The mystery (μυστήριον) was made known to him by revelation: Gentiles are fellow heirs, members of the same body, and partakers of the promise in Christ Jesus through the gospel.

children.text: This mystery was not made known to the sons of men in other generations as it has now been revealed to the apostles and prophets by the Spirit. The 'as' (ὡς) is comparative — not that it was totally unknown in the OT, but the full inclusion of Gentiles as co-heirs was not disclosed with this clarity.

nodes.text: Paul's commission: to preach to the Gentiles the unsearchable riches of Christ, and to make everyone see the plan of the mystery hidden for ages in God. The purpose: so that through the church the manifold wisdom (πολυποίκιλος σοφία) of God might now be made known to the rulers and authorities in the heavenly places.

children.text: The church's existence is a cosmic announcement — the rulers and authorities in heavenly places (including hostile powers) learn of God's wisdom by witnessing the multi-ethnic, reconciled community. The church is not merely the beneficiary of the gospel but its cosmic demonstration.

In Christ we have boldness and access with confidence through our faith in him. Paul's sufferings are not cause for discouragement — they are the Ephesians' glory. Apostolic suffering participates in the pattern of Christ's cross that produces the church's life.

nodes.text: The intercession resumes (picking up from v. 1). Paul bows before the Father — from whom every family in heaven and on earth is named — with four petitions: (1) strength in the inner being through the Spirit; (2) Christ dwelling (κατοικεῖν) in their hearts through faith; (3) being rooted and grounded in love to comprehend with all the saints the dimensions of Christ's love; (4) being filled with all the fullness of God (πλήρωμα τοῦ θεοῦ).

children.text: The four dimensions (breadth, length, height, depth) of Christ's love — which surpasses knowledge. The paradox 'know the love that surpasses knowledge' is intentional: only experiential, Spirit-empowered knowledge of love is adequate. This is not mere doctrinal understanding but transformative encounter.

nodes.text: Doxology closing the letter's first half: to him who is able to do far more abundantly than all we ask or think, according to the power at work within us — to him be glory in the church and in Christ Jesus throughout all generations. The 'power at work within us' echoes 1:20 — the resurrection power is the same power now operative in the community of faith.

children.text: Glory in the church and in Christ Jesus: the two loci of divine glory are parallel. The church is not merely a vehicle for Christ's glory — it is, together with Christ, the sphere in which God's glory is displayed.

discourse.note: Ephesians 3 is one sustained movement from mystery revealed (vv. 1-13) to prayer for its internalization (vv. 14-19) to doxology for the power that accomplishes it (vv. 20-21). The 'manifold wisdom' (πολυποίκιλος — many-colored, multi-faceted) is language used in the LXX of Joseph's coat and in Greek literature for elaborate embroidery — God's wisdom displayed through the church is breathtakingly complex and beautiful.

coaching.tip: Paul was made a servant of 'the mystery' (v. 3)—that Gentiles are co-heirs with Israel in Christ. What was hidden for ages is now disclosed. Paul's prison cell becomes the stage where cosmic revelation unfolds. Even 'rulers and authorities in heavenly realms' (v. 10) learn God's wisdom through the church.

Paul prays they would grasp 'how wide and long and high and deep is the love of Christ' (v. 18)—four dimensions to describe something beyond dimensions. The prayer climaxes: 'filled to the measure of all the fullness of God' (v. 19). Hyperbole? Or the actual Christian destiny?
